Bug Bounty Write-up · Business Logic

Matterport Free Plan User Limit Bypass via RestoreMembership API

During my research on Matterport, I discovered a business logic vulnerability that allowed a Free Plan organization administrator to restore previously removed users through a GraphQL API, bypassing the user limit enforced by the free subscription tier.

Target: MatterportType: Subscription Enforcement BypassComponent: GraphQL APIImpact: Revenue / Billing Logic

Background

Matterport's Free Plan limits the number of members in an organization. Once the limit is reached, the UI displays a user-limit warning and asks the administrator to remove existing users or upgrade the plan.

Discovery

While monitoring frontend requests, I identified the RestoreMembership mutation. This functionality was triggered when an administrator attempted to reactivate a previously removed user.

POST /api/mp/accounts/graph?operation=RestoreMembership&organizationId=<ORG_ID>
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "operationName": "RestoreMembership",
  "variables": {
    "organizationId": "<ORG_ID>",
    "email": "removed-user@example.com",
    "userGroupsPageSize": 50
  },
  "query": "mutation RestoreMembership($organizationId: ID!, $email: String!, $userGroupsPageSize: Int) { restoreMembership(organizationId: $organizationId, email: $email) { status user { id email firstName lastName } roles { name id } } }"
}

Root Cause

The frontend correctly checked the organization quota and blocked the restore action when the Free Plan user limit was reached. However, the backend GraphQL mutation restoreMembership did not validate whether the current number of active users was below the subscription limit.

Expected server-side check:
Current active users < Subscription user limit

Actual behavior:
RestoreMembership still returns HTTP 200 and status = active

Proof of Concept

Step 1 — Reach the Free Plan limit

The organization was on the Free Plan with a 2-user limit. After reaching or exceeding the quota, the UI displayed a user-limit warning.

Step 2 — Reactivating a user through the UI is blocked

When attempting to reactivate a disinvited user through the UI, the application displayed a modal asking the administrator to remove users or upgrade the plan.

Step 3 — Call the RestoreMembership API directly

Instead of using the UI, an attacker can send a direct request to the GraphQL endpoint with the email address of the removed user.

Impact

  • Bypass the billing/subscription restriction of the Free Plan.
  • Allow an organization to use more active users than permitted by its plan.
  • Create an inconsistent state between the frontend billing limit and backend membership state.
  • Potential revenue loss if users maintain extra active members without upgrading the plan.

Recommended Fix

The backend should enforce quota checks on every mutation that can increase the number of active users, including invite, restore, reactivate, bulk import, or any other membership-related flow.

Before restoring a membership:
1. Load organization subscription plan
2. Count current active users
3. Compare with allowed user quota
4. Reject restore if active_users >= quota
5. Return a clear error code, for example USER_LIMIT_REACHED

Takeaway

This is a classic SaaS business logic issue: any limit related to billing, entitlement, or subscription must be enforced on the backend. The frontend should only support the user experience, not act as the final security control.

Written by Nhan Tieu · Bug bounty / API Security / Business Logic Research
